title: Move Semantics 🚚
description: When does data get copied vs moved between variables?

slides.title: The Classic Copy 📝
The String Type 🧶

quizData.question: What happens when you assign a Heap-based variable (`s1`) to another variable (`s2`)?

options.text: Both variables safely point to the data
The first variable (`s1`) becomes permanently invalid
Rust automatically clones the entire heap memory

quizData.explanation: Due to Ownership Rule #2 (There can only be ONE owner at a time), `s1` MUST instantly become invalid otherwise memory would be freed twice when they both go out of scope.

slides.title: Moves, not Copies 🚚
slides.content:
Why does this error happen?

Because there can only be **one owner**. When you type `let s2 = s1;`, Rust copies the *Pointer* (on the Stack), but does NOT copy the giant Heap data.

If `s1` and `s2` both lived to the end of the scope, they would both try to delete the exact same Heap memory (a deadly 'double free' error). 

So Rust simply declares `s1` dead immediately. We say the data was **Moved**!

slides.title: Cloning 🐑
slides.content:
What if we *actually* want to deeply copy the Heap data so we have two safe variables?

You explicitly call `.clone()`!

```rust
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let s2 = s1.clone();
println!("{} and {}", s1, s2); // Works perfectly!
```

quizData.question: What is the key difference between moving and cloning a String?

options.text: Moving is an error. Cloning is the default.
Moving simply transfers the pointer (fast) and invalidates the old variable. Cloning completely duplicates the heap data (slow).

quizData.explanation: Because duplicating a huge Heap object could aggressively drag down performance, Rust never automatically clones. It strictly **moves** the pointer instead.
